transcript: Hi, my name is Lana, and these are some of the lessons that I've learned from traveling. My journeys are far from over, but here are some of my reflections. People are kinder than you think. I started this journey as a solo traveler in Singapore. Thinking about being alone on my travels was something that made me afraid and excited at the same time. What if something went wrong? What if I got lost? What if I make no friends? But on my first day of solo travelling, I met two girls at my hostel. By the next day, I had met four people already and I was going off on adventures with a girl I had only just met from England. It was like being at primary school again. The day before this, I had bought a bottle of water at the convenience store down the road. The next day I walked by and the shop owner came running out to me to hand the 10 cents change that I had forgotten. 10 cents that I didn't even realise I had left behind. But it was just the morale of giving back something that could have easily been overlooked that made me realise this solo adventure wasn't going to be as intimidating as I feared. People can adapt and find happiness in most situations. Travelling through Southeast Asia was an eye-opener. I met children who were happy just to play in a pile of dirt with a string and a dog. It amazed me that these children looked more content than some of the children that I knew back home who owned iPads, cell phones and all of the latest technologies. It made me think that we really don't need many possessions to be happy. People choose stability over happiness. I learned that if I wanted to live life to the fullest, I needed to do what I wanted to do. After working as a nurse for a few years, I realized that I still wasn't finding fulfillment and just becoming comfortable because I thought that it would benefit my future. We all want to be happy and I believe that your level of freedom significantly affects your happiness. When my partner Carlos suggested teaching English online, I dived straight into it. Teaching has allowed me to pursue more meaningful goals and figure out what I really want to do in life. It felt risky to take the leap and travel the world, but I'd rather make uncomfortable decisions and lead the life that I want to, than to be comfortable and unfulfilled. Solo travel doesn't always mean that you will be alone. Despite being quite a shy person, backpacking solo I met people from all across the world. That's even how I met my partner. You'd be surprised at how easy it is to meet people solo traveling. Some days you'll find yourself around people so much that you'll end up wanting time alone. If you're waiting for someone to travel with you, you may end up waiting a lifetime. Bucket list items are certainly a great reason to visit a place, but it's usually the people you meet that help create warm memories of your travels It was terrific to visit Marina Bay Sands Hotel in Singapore or Disneyland in Tokyo Still for me the most memorable times during traveling have always been accompanied by friends or the locals that I've met along the way Research a place before you go It's always better to be over-prepared than under-prepared when arriving in a new country. Usually people are friendly and willing to help you, but speaking as a solo traveller, it's good to practice preparation and a decent level of situational awareness. Research cost of living, the distance of your accommodation to transport, SIM cards and popular scams in the area. All of this can save you a lot of time and money. Public transport can be daunting at times but it's often cheaper to figure this out than to spend a load of money on taxis or Ubers. Travelling is more affordable than you think. I know before travelling as a solo backpacker, I felt that travelling meant staying at expensive hotels and paying a ridiculous amount of money for a guided tour. But most of these things can be done on a budget. You can get a private room in Vietnam for about $20 a night and staying at hostels is even cheaper. The cheapest I visited was about two dollars a night. Southeast Asia is a backpackers dream. Of course everyone likes to travel differently but knowing where the budget-friendly countries to travel are makes the options endless for you. Even if you do like that private tour guided experience there are countries where you can get this for about $70 a day, as compared to $400 a day. Money is a tool to create the life that you want. Think about the things in life that really make you happy, like having the freedom to spend more time with your friends and family. For me, it was having the time to go wherever I wanted. Now that I teach and travel, I've chosen to work fewer hours than I usually would as a nurse. Although my pay is much less, I don't feel the need to spend money on items like clothing or makeup. And since I travel full time, I don't have room for it in my bag either. My spendings usually go towards Airbnbs, transport and food. Since we travel to the low cost of living countries I tend to save more than I did back home. Having the freedom to choose where we want to go is because we treat money as a tool and not as the end goal. I couldn't understand or appreciate my own nation until I left it. New Zealand is viewed as a clean green wanderlust sort of country to visitors. Of course I knew this before but I don't think I understood how great we have it in New Zealand until I started traveling and experiencing other cultures and countries. I am so proud to be a Kiwi and grateful for the opportunities that I had growing up as a kid. I can travel anywhere in the world and New Zealand will always feel like home to me. Planning everything is sometimes more of a hassle than a help. At the beginning of my travels I used to have everything booked from flights to accommodation but then I started meeting people who told me about unique places that I hadn't booked for and I couldn't join in on those adventures since it would cost me to cancel my other bookings. Of course, I believe that preparation is key to arrival in a new country, but sometimes not knowing everything about a place is exciting, and not booking a return flight allows you more time to experience something that you didn't plan for. Disorientation is beautiful. remember to be present. We live in a digital age where it's so common to get lost in our social media and we forget to enjoy the moment. It's not to say you shouldn't capture these important memories but taking the time to appreciate that sunset or that beautiful landscape means more to me than a photo I took for Instagram that someone will just swipe past. Capture moments but don't forget to connect. Thanks for watching and I hope that this video has inspired you to explore in some form or another so stay tuned to catch more travel videos like this one.
